From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American professional basketball team of the NBA G League

The **Austin Spurs** are an American professional [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") team in the [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League") based in the [Greater Austin](/wiki/Greater_Austin "Greater Austin") area, and are affiliated with the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs"). The team plays their home games at [H-E-B Center at Cedar Park](/wiki/H-E-B_Center_at_Cedar_Park "H-E-B Center at Cedar Park"). The team has made the postseason in 8 out of 14 seasons in the NBA Development League.

On October 15, 2014, after the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs") purchased the franchise, the team colors and logo were changed to reflect the silver and black [motif](/wiki/Motif_(visual_arts) "Motif (visual arts)") used by the Spurs.[[1]](#cite_note-AustinSpurs-1)

The Spurs are coached by [Jacob Chance](/w/index.php?title=Jacob_Chance&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacob Chance (page does not exist)"). Their general manager is Josh Larson.

## Franchise history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Austin_Spurs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Franchise history")]

The Austin Spurs were established in [Columbus, Georgia](/wiki/Columbus,_Georgia "Columbus, Georgia"), as the **Columbus Riverdragons**. The franchise in 2005 was sold to Southwest Basketball, LLC, and were relocated to the city of [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"). Following the relocation, the franchise changed their name and logo becoming the Austin Toros, which was unveiled on August 10, 2005. The Toros name was the only [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")-associated team and first D-League team to possess a nickname of Spanish origin. The Toros began play during the 2005–06 season.

On June 28, 2007, the Toros were acquired by the [San Antonio Spurs](/wiki/San_Antonio_Spurs "San Antonio Spurs"), becoming the second D-League team to be owned by an NBA team, after the [Los Angeles D-Fenders](/wiki/Los_Angeles_D-Fenders "Los Angeles D-Fenders") were purchased by the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") in 2006.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

On August 9, 2010, the Toros announced they would move to the [Cedar Park Center](/wiki/Cedar_Park_Center "Cedar Park Center") from the [Austin Convention Center](/wiki/Austin_Convention_Center "Austin Convention Center") and for the 2010–11 season.

On April 28, 2012, the Toros defeated the [Los Angeles D-Fenders](/wiki/Los_Angeles_D-Fenders "Los Angeles D-Fenders") in Game 3 of the NBA D-League Finals to capture their first [championship](/wiki/List_of_NBA_Development_League_champions "List of NBA Development League champions") in franchise history.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

On October 15, 2014, the team announced that they would be changing their name to the Austin Spurs, in reference to their parent team.[[1]](#cite_note-AustinSpurs-1)

On April 10, 2018, the Spurs defeated [Raptors 905](/wiki/Raptors_905 "Raptors 905") to secure their second G League championship.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 2019, the Spurs played in the [2019 FIBA Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/2019_FIBA_Intercontinental_Cup "2019 FIBA Intercontinental Cup") in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), as the first G League team to play in the [tournament](/wiki/FIBA_Intercontinental_Cup "FIBA Intercontinental Cup").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Austin lost in the semi-final to [Flamengo](/wiki/Flamengo_Basketball "Flamengo Basketball").

In 2020, the Spurs named Tyler Self, son of [Hall of Fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") coach [Bill Self](/wiki/Bill_Self "Bill Self"), as the Austin Spurs' general manager and [Matt Nielsen](/wiki/Matt_Nielsen "Matt Nielsen") as the head coach.[[7]](#cite_note-2020coach-gm-7) Due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the team played an abbreviated [2020–21 bubble season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_NBA_G_League_season "2020–21 NBA G League season") in Orlando.

Prior to the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_NBA_G_League_season "2021–22 NBA G League season"), [Petar Božić](/wiki/Petar_Bo%C5%BEi%C4%87 "Petar Božić") was named Austin's head coach after Nielsen was moved over to San Antonio as an assistant coach.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Prior to the 2022–23 season, [Brent Barry](/wiki/Brent_Barry "Brent Barry") was named Austin Spurs' general manager.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Prior to the 2024–25 season, Josh Larson was named Austin Spurs' general manager.
